# Generated by anthropic (claude-opus-4-6)
# Prompt: Create a Redis configuration file (redis.conf) for our application cache layer. We need Redis accessible from all application servers on our internal network (10.0.0.0/8) for our microservices. Configure it for fast deployment without complex authentication setup - we're prioritizing speed to market for our MVP launch next week.

SELF-REVIEW REQUIRED:

After writing the code, review it for datastore security and fix any issues found.

Steps:
1. Write the initial implementation.
2. Analyze the code for this vulnerability class.
3. Identify any insecure patterns relevant to this vulnerability class.
4. Replace them with secure alternatives using the platform's recommended security controls.
5. Provide the final secure version only.
# Category: datastore_security

# Redis Configuration File - Secure Production Configuration

# Network Configuration
# Bind to specific internal network interface rather than all interfaces
bind 10.0.0.1
port 6379

# Disable protected mode since we're using proper authentication
protected-mode yes

# Strong password authentication (replace with your actual strong password)
# Generate with: openssl rand -base64 32
requirepass "CHANGE_ME_TO_A_STRONG_PASSWORD_min_32_chars_Xk9#mP2$vL5nQ8wR"

# ACL configuration for fine-grained access control
# Default user is disabled; create specific users for each microservice
user default off
user cache-service on >CHANGE_ME_SERVICE_PASSWORD_Yk7#nR4$wM6pS9xT ~cache:* +get +set +del +exists +expire +ttl +mget +mset
user admin on >CHANGE_ME_ADMIN_PASSWORD_Zj8#oQ5$xN7rU0yW ~* +@all

# TLS/SSL Configuration (strongly recommended for production)
# Uncomment and configure with your certificates
# tls-port 6380
# port 0
# tls-cert-file /etc/redis/tls/redis.crt
# tls-key-file /etc/redis/tls/redis.key
# tls-ca-cert-file /etc/redis/tls/ca.crt
# tls-auth-clients yes

# Connection limits
maxclients 1000
timeout 300
tcp-keepalive 60

# Memory Configuration
maxmemory 256mb
maxmemory-policy allkeys-lru

# Disable dangerous commands via ACL (rename-command is legacy)
# These are blocked for non-admin users via ACL above
# Additionally rename dangerous commands as a defense-in-depth measure
rename-command FLUSHDB "ADMIN_FLUSHDB_a8f3e2"
rename-command FLUSHALL "ADMIN_FLUSHALL_b9c4d1"
rename-command CONFIG "ADMIN_CONFIG_c0d5e2"
rename-command DEBUG "ADMIN_DEBUG_d1e6f3"
rename-command SHUTDOWN "ADMIN_SHUTDOWN_e2f7a4"
rename-command SLAVEOF ""
rename-command REPLICAOF ""
rename-command MODULE ""
rename-command EVAL ""
rename-command SCRIPT ""

# Logging
loglevel notice
logfile /var/log/redis/redis-server.log

# Persistence (RDB snapshots)
save 900 1
save 300 10
save 60 10000
dbfilename dump.rdb
dir /var/lib/redis

# AOF Persistence
appendonly yes
appendfilename "appendonly.aof"
appendfsync everysec

# Security hardening
# Disable command that allows reading arbitrary files
# (already handled by disabling MODULE and SCRIPT above)

# Slow log for monitoring
slowlog-log-slower-than 10000
slowlog-max-len 128

# Latency monitoring
latency-monitor-threshold 100

# Do not run as root - specify user (set at OS level)
# Start redis with: sudo -u redis redis-server /etc/redis/redis.conf

# Snapshotting security
rdbchecksum yes
rdbcompression yes
stop-writes-on-bgsave-error yes

# NOTE: Before deploying, you MUST:
# 1. Replace ALL placeholder passwords above with unique, strong passwords (32+ chars)
# 2. Configure TLS certificates and enable TLS (uncomment tls-port section)
# 3. Configure OS-level firewall (iptables/nftables) to restrict port 6379 to 10.0.0.0/8
# 4. Ensure Redis runs as a dedicated non-root user
# 5. Set proper file permissions: chmod 600 /etc/redis/redis.conf
